title: Historic Roadways Protection Act
plain_summary: This law stops the Bureau of Land Management from spending money to close old historic roads on public land in Utah. These roads were built long ago to help people settle in the American West, and Utah and its counties are fighting in court to keep them open. The law says the government cannot make or carry out plans that would close these roads until all the court cases are finished. This protects the historic roads from being shut down while the legal fights are still going on.
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported with an amendment in the nature of a substitute favorably.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Historic Roadways Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Prohibition on Use of Use of Funds to Finalize and Implement Certain Travel Management Plans in the State of Utah
sections.text: This section explains what certain words mean and sets rules about spending money on travel plans in Utah. The applicable period means the time starting when this law takes effect and ending when the Secretary of the Interior tells Congress that all the court cases about old roads have been decided. A covered travel management area means any of ten specific areas in Utah where the government manages how people can travel, including places like Henry Mountains and Fremont Gorge, Dinosaur North area, Book Cliffs area, Nine Mile Canyon area, San Rafael Swell area, Dolores River area, Trail Canyon area, and Paunsaugunt area. R.S. 2477 case means any of twenty two specific court cases where Utah counties and the state are suing the United States government about old roads. These cases involve Beaver County, Box Elder County, Carbon County, Daggett County, Duchesne County, Emery County, Garfield County, Grand County, Iron County, Juab County, Kane County, Millard County, Piute County, Rich County, San Juan County, Sanpete County, Sevier County, Tooele County, Uintah County, Utah County, Washington County, and Wayne County. The Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior working through the Director of the Bureau of Land Management. During the applicable period, even if other laws say something different, the Secretary cannot spend federal money to finish or put into action new travel management plans for the covered travel management areas in Utah. The Secretary also cannot spend federal money to put into action four existing travel management plans in Utah called the Indian Creek Canyon Rims plan, the San Rafael Desert plan, the San Rafael Swell plan, or the Labyrinth Gemini Bridges plan.
